title: Officers fired for alleged Flock camera misuse in DeKalb, Henry, Fayetteville
excerpt: Police say the license plate readers help locate suspects and solve crimes, but a growing number of critics say the technology amounts to an invasion of privacy.
content_html:
UPDATE

Fayetteville police terminated three officers after an audit of its surveillance system technology.

The former law enforcement personnel are among a growing list of Georgia officers who have been fired or criminally charged for allegedly misusing the widespread surveillance technology. (AJC File)

Updated July 18, 2026

A DeKalb County Sheriff’s Office deputy has been fired and also faces criminal charges after authorities said he misused the agency’s license plate reader system.

Additionally, three Fayetteville police officers are out of a job after the department said Friday they looked up their own tag numbers or those belonging to people they know.

They join a growing list of Georgia law enforcement officers who have recently been terminated or criminally charged for allegedly misusing the widespread surveillance technology.

In DeKalb, former Sgt. Kabiru Salawu, a nearly 17-year sheriff’s office veteran, was arrested Friday and charged with a felony violation of oath by a public officer and misuse of license plate data, a misdemeanor, according to the agency.

The sheriff’s office said the unauthorized use was uncovered following an internal investigation, which it did not detail in a news release.

“The misuse of law enforcement technology or confidential information for any unauthorized purpose is a serious breach of the public’s trust and will not be tolerated. When misconduct is identified, we act swiftly, hold those responsible accountable, and remain committed to the highest ethical standards,” Sheriff Melody M. Maddox said in a statement.

Salawu, 55, was booked into the DeKalb jail Friday evening and remained in custody Saturday morning, according to online records.

The Fayetteville officers, whose names were not disclosed, were fired after their unauthorized searches were discovered “during the activation and testing of a new internal auditing feature,” Fayetteville police said.

As has happened in other cases, Fayetteville’s department said auditing tools meant to strengthen oversight of the surveillance system flagged the police officers’ activity.

An internal investigation was launched after the audit “identified what appeared to be license plate searches inconsistent with the system’s authorized use,” the Fayetteville police department said in a news release announcing the firings.

A spokesperson declined to name the three former officers or say how long they had been with the department.

“The activity identified during the audit revealed that the employees searched their own license plates or those of a family member/acquaintance, and not random members of the public,” the department said, noting the searches were made with no “legitimate law-enforcement purpose.”

While no charges have been filed, a GBI spokesperson confirmed Fayetteville police alerted the state agency, which is now investigating.

Flock Safety, the Atlanta-based manufacturer of the license-plate readers, has come under immense scrutiny in recent months over what critics view as an invasive surveillance system knitted together through a network of cameras frequently mounted on poles, streetlights and overpasses.

Some cities have abandoned their Flock contracts amid the growing concerns about what is done with the data collected.

Flock in April rolled out a new “audit assistance” feature that automatically flags searches that seem suspicious and alerts a police department’s leaders, the company told The Atlanta Journal-Constitution last week.

An off-duty officer searching Flock’s database would be an example of the type of activity that could trigger a notification.

The new feature is available to all police departments that use Flock, but for now, it is something they have to choose to turn on, the company said. It wasn’t immediately clear if Fayetteville police were using the new feature when the department discovered the searches that led to the recent firings.

Flock has an estimated 140,000 active users accessing data each month and says the majority of those searches are appropriate.

“We trust law enforcement to do the right thing. And the vast, vast majority of them do,” said Josh Thomas, chief communications officer for Flock. “(But) if there’s corruption within the police department, that needs to be outed and those people need to be held accountable.”

Flock’s network includes more than 120,000 cameras across 49 states, according to the company’s website. Those license plate readers make an estimated 20 billion scans each month, capturing the license plate number and other characteristics of every vehicle that passes by its cameras.

At least seven other Georgia law enforcement officers were fired this month and charged with misusing the technology.

That includes a Henry County police officer who was fired and arrested last week following an investigation into his access of the Flock network, authorities there said.

Patrick Isbell, 41, was charged with misuse of license plate data on July 10 after an internal audit found he accessed the department’s system and “utilized the retained license plate data for non-law enforcement purposes,” an agency spokesperson said. That investigation is ongoing.

Isbell, in an email to the AJC, declined to comment on the specifics of his case but said he believes “there are substantial and legitimate questions” regarding his former department’s compliance with the law that regulates license plate readers.

“This is an evolving area of law, technology, privacy, and public accountability. When an agency relies on a statute to pursue criminal charges against an employee, the public has a legitimate interest in determining whether that agency followed the same law consistently and responsibly,” he said.

In June, three Cherokee County sheriff’s deputies and a Richmond County deputy were arrested after allegedly using the departments’ license plate recognition databases for non-law enforcement purposes. In November, the city of Braselton’s then-police chief resigned after the GBI said he misused the systems to harass and stalk multiple people.

Similar cases outside metro Atlanta include a Coffee County deputy accused of misusing license plate data and stalking, and an Echols County Sheriff’s Office secretary accused of using the agency’s Flock account to search tag information for two people she knew.

Fayetteville police called the license plate reader technology “an important public safety resource” that helps officers track down stolen cars, identify vehicles connected to criminal investigations and find missing people.

“Access to this technology carries a significant responsibility, and department policy strictly prohibits its use for any personal purpose,” the agency said in its statement.

Flock has long argued its technology helps deter crime because would-be criminals know they are being watched. Thomas said that same logic applies to police misuse of the data.

“If cops know that they will be caught, they are not going to commit these crimes,” he said.

— Staff writer Vanessa McCray contributed to this article.

sentences.zh: “这是一个法律、技术、隐私和公共责任不断发展的领域。

sentences.en: When an agency relies on a statute to pursue criminal charges against an employee, the public has a legitimate interest in determining whether that agency followed the same law consistently and responsibly,” he said.
sentences.zh: 当一个机构依据法规对雇员提出刑事指控时，公众有合法利益来确定该机构是否始终如一且负责任地遵守同一法律，”他说。

blocks.html_en: In June, three Cherokee County sheriff’s deputies and a Richmond County deputy were arrested after allegedly using the departments’ license plate recognition databases for non-law enforcement purposes. In November, the city of Braselton’s then-police chief resigned after the GBI said he misused the systems to harass and stalk multiple people.

sentences.en: In June, three Cherokee County sheriff’s deputies and a Richmond County deputy were arrested after allegedly using the departments’ license plate recognition databases for non-law enforcement purposes.
sentences.zh: 六月，三名切罗基县警长和一名里士满县警员因涉嫌将部门的车牌识别数据库用于非执法目的而被捕。

sentences.en: In November, the city of Braselton’s then-police chief resigned after the GBI said he misused the systems to harass and stalk multiple people.
sentences.zh: 去年 11 月，时任布拉塞尔顿市警察局长在 GBI 称他滥用这些系统骚扰和跟踪多人后辞职。

blocks.html_en: Similar cases outside metro Atlanta include a Coffee County deputy accused of misusing license plate data and stalking, and an Echols County Sheriff’s Office secretary accused of using the agency’s Flock account to search tag information for two people she knew.

sentences.en: Similar cases outside metro Atlanta include a Coffee County deputy accused of misusing license plate data and stalking, and an Echols County Sheriff’s Office secretary accused of using the agency’s Flock account to search tag information for two people she knew.
sentences.zh: 亚特兰大都会区以外的类似案件包括一名咖啡县警官被指控滥用车牌数据和跟踪，以及一名埃科尔斯县治安官办公室秘书被指控使用该机构的 Flock 帐户搜索她认识的两个人的标签信息。

blocks.html_en: Fayetteville police called the license plate reader technology “an important public safety resource” that helps officers track down stolen cars, identify vehicles connected to criminal investigations and find missing people.

sentences.en: Fayetteville police called the license plate reader technology “an important public safety resource” that helps officers track down stolen cars, identify vehicles connected to criminal investigations and find missing people.
sentences.zh: 费耶特维尔警方称车牌读取器技术是“重要的公共安全资源”，可帮助警察追查被盗汽车、识别与刑事调查有关的车辆并寻找失踪人员。

blocks.html_en: “Access to this technology carries a significant responsibility, and department policy strictly prohibits its use for any personal purpose,” the agency said in its statement.

sentences.en: “Access to this technology carries a significant responsibility, and department policy strictly prohibits its use for any personal purpose,” the agency said in its statement.
sentences.zh: 该机构在声明中表示：“使用这项技术需要承担重大责任，部门政策严格禁止将其用于任何个人目的。”

blocks.html_en: Flock has long argued its technology helps deter crime because would-be criminals know they are being watched. Thomas said that same logic applies to police misuse of the data.

sentences.en: Flock has long argued its technology helps deter crime because would-be criminals know they are being watched.
sentences.zh: 弗洛克长期以来一直认为其技术有助于阻止犯罪，因为潜在的犯罪分子知道他们正在受到监视。

sentences.en: Thomas said that same logic applies to police misuse of the data.
sentences.zh: 托马斯说，同样的逻辑也适用于警方滥用数据。

blocks.html_en: “If cops know that they will be caught, they are not going to commit these crimes,” he said.

sentences.en: “If cops know that they will be caught, they are not going to commit these crimes,” he said.
sentences.zh: “如果警察知道他们会被抓住，他们就不会犯下这些罪行，”他说。

blocks.html_en: — Staff writer Vanessa McCray contributed to this article.

sentences.en: — Staff writer Vanessa McCray contributed to this article.
sentences.zh: — 特约撰稿人凡妮莎·麦克雷 (Vanessa McCray) 对本文做出了贡献。

content_html_zh:
更新

费耶特维尔警方在对其监控系统技术进行审核后解雇了三名警官。

越来越多的佐治亚州官员因涉嫌滥用广泛的监控技术而被解雇或受到刑事指控，这些前执法人员就是其中之一。 （AJC 文件）

更新于 2026 年 7 月 18 日

迪卡尔布县治安官办公室的一名副手已被解雇，并因当局称他滥用该机构的车牌读取系统而面临刑事指控。

此外，费耶特维尔警察局周五表示，他们查询了自己的标签号码或属于他们认识的人的标签号码后，三名费耶特维尔警察因此失业。

他们加入了越来越多的佐治亚州执法人员行列，这些执法人员最近因涉嫌滥用广泛的监控技术而被解雇或受到刑事指控。

在迪卡尔布，前中士。 据该机构称，卡比鲁·萨拉乌 (Kabiru Salawu) 是一名在警长办公室任职近 17 年的资深人士，于周五被捕，并被指控犯有公职人员违反誓言的重罪和滥用车牌数据的轻罪。

治安官办公室表示，经过内部调查后，发现了未经授权的使用行为，但并未在新闻稿中详细说明这一情况。

“出于任何未经授权的目的滥用执法技术或机密信息严重违反了公众的信任，是不会容忍的。 当发现不当行为时，我们会迅速采取行动，追究责任人的责任，并继续致力于最高的道德标准。” 马多克斯在一份声明中说道。

根据网上记录，55 岁的萨拉乌周五晚上被关押在迪卡尔布监狱，周六早上仍被拘留。

费耶特维尔警方表示，这些未透露姓名的费耶特维尔警官在“新的内部审计功能启动和测试期间”发现未经授权的搜查行为后被解雇。

与其他案件一样，费耶特维尔部门表示，旨在加强对监控系统监督的审计工具标记了警察的活动。

费耶特维尔警察局在宣布解雇的新闻稿中表示，审计“发现车牌搜查似乎与系统授权使用不符”后，启动了内部调查。

一位发言人拒绝透露这三名前警官的姓名，也拒绝透露他们在该部门工作了多久。

该部门表示：“审计期间发现的活动显示，员工搜查了自己或家庭成员/熟人的车牌，而不是随机的公众成员。”并指出，搜查的目的没有“合法的执法目的”。

虽然尚未提出任何指控，但 GBI 发言人证实，费耶特维尔警方已向国家机构发出警报，该机构目前正在调查。

总部位于亚特兰大的车牌阅读器制造商 Flock Safety 近几个月受到了严厉的审查，因为批评者认为该系统是一种侵入性监控系统，该系统通过经常安装在电线杆、路灯和立交桥上的摄像头网络编织而成。

由于人们越来越担心收集到的数据会被用来做什么，一些城市已经放弃了 Flock 合同。

该公司上周告诉《亚特兰大宪法报》，Flock 在四月份推出了一项新的“审计协助”功能，可以自动标记可疑的搜索并向警察部门的领导发出警报。

下班的官员搜索 Flock 的数据库就是可能触发通知的活动类型的一个例子。

该公司表示，所有使用 Flock 的警察部门都可以使用这项新功能，但目前他们必须选择打开该功能。 目前还不清楚费耶特维尔警方在发现导致最近解雇事件的搜查行为时是否正在使用这一新功能。

Flock 每月估计有 140,000 名活跃用户访问数据，并表示其中大部分搜索都是合适的。

“我们相信执法部门会做正确的事。 绝大多数人都是这么做的，”Flock 首席通讯官乔什·托马斯 (Josh Thomas) 说。 “（但是）如果警察部门内部存在腐败行为，就需要将其揭发，并追究这些人的责任。”

据该公司网站称，Flock 的网络包括遍布 49 个州的 120,000 多个摄像头。 这些车牌读取器每月估计进行 200 亿次扫描，捕获经过其摄像头的每辆车的车牌号和其他特征。

本月，至少有七名佐治亚州执法人员被解雇，并被指控滥用该技术。

亨利县当局表示，其中包括一名亨利县警官，他在对 Flock 网络访问情况进行调查后于上周被解雇并被捕。

该机构发言人表示，41 岁的帕特里克·伊斯贝尔 (Patrick Isbell) 于 7 月 10 日被指控滥用车牌数据，此前内部审计发现他访问了该部门的系统，并“将保留的车牌数据用于非执法目的”。 该调查正在进行中。

伊斯贝尔在发给 AJC 的电子邮件中拒绝就其案件的具体细节发表评论，但表示他认为他的前部门遵守监管车牌读取器的法律的情况“存在实质性且合理的问题”。

“这是一个法律、技术、隐私和公共责任不断发展的领域。 当一个机构依据法规对雇员提出刑事指控时，公众有合法利益来确定该机构是否始终如一且负责任地遵守同一法律，”他说。

六月，三名切罗基县警长和一名里士满县警员因涉嫌将部门的车牌识别数据库用于非执法目的而被捕。 去年 11 月，时任布拉塞尔顿市警察局长在 GBI 称他滥用这些系统骚扰和跟踪多人后辞职。

亚特兰大都会区以外的类似案件包括一名咖啡县警官被指控滥用车牌数据和跟踪，以及一名埃科尔斯县治安官办公室秘书被指控使用该机构的 Flock 帐户搜索她认识的两个人的标签信息。

费耶特维尔警方称车牌读取器技术是“重要的公共安全资源”，可帮助警察追查被盗汽车、识别与刑事调查有关的车辆并寻找失踪人员。

该机构在声明中表示：“使用这项技术需要承担重大责任，部门政策严格禁止将其用于任何个人目的。”

弗洛克长期以来一直认为其技术有助于阻止犯罪，因为潜在的犯罪分子知道他们正在受到监视。 托马斯说，同样的逻辑也适用于警方滥用数据。

“如果警察知道他们会被抓住，他们就不会犯下这些罪行，”他说。

— 特约撰稿人凡妮莎·麦克雷 (Vanessa McCray) 对本文做出了贡献。
